cl: Struggle of the Orders (patricians vs. plebeians)
Roman expansion in Italy (Latin, Samnite, Pyrrhic wars)
Conquest of Mediterranean (Punic Wars, Macedonia, Syria)
Fall of Republic: Pompey, Crassus, Cicero, Catiline
Year of the Four Emperors (69 CE) & Flavian dynasty
Five Good Emperors (Nerva to Marcus Aurelius)
Late Empire & fall of the West (395–476 CE)
Social hierarchy: senators, equestrians, freedmen, slaves

units.nt: Covers Apennine mountains, Tiber River, Latium region; early inhabitants: Latins, Etruscans, Greeks, Sabines; influence of Etruscan civilization on early Rome; excludes detailed toponymy of each tribe.
Covers legend of twins, she-wolf, founding date 753 BCE; Aeneas as Trojan refugee and link to Homeric epic; historical vs mythical interpretation; excludes alternative foundation stories.

units.n: The Regal Period (753–509 BCE): Seven Kings
units.nt: Covers traditional list of kings, contributions (institutions, temples, Servian reforms); transition from monarchy to republic; historical reliability of king list; excludes detailed genealogy.

units.n: Overthrow of Tarquinius Superbus & Early Republic Institutions
units.nt: Covers rape of Lucretia, expulsion of last king; creation of consuls, Senate, comitia centuriata; early conflict with Etruscan cities; excludes detailed legislative procedures.

units.n: Struggle of the Orders: Patricians vs. Plebeians
units.nt: Covers secessions of the plebs (494, 449, 287 BCE); tribunes of the plebs, Twelve Tables; lex Hortensia (287 BCE); achievement of political equality; excludes detailed legal reforms.
Covers Latin League, Samnite Wars (343–290 BCE), Pyrrhic War (280–275 BCE); tactics of Roman manipular legion; road building (Via Appia); excludes detailed battle narratives.

units.n: The Punic Wars (264–146 BCE): Rome vs. Carthage
units.nt: Covers First (naval, Sicily), Second (Hannibal, Cannae, Scipio), Third (Carthage destroyed); key generals: Hamilcar, Hannibal, Scipio Africanus; political consequences; excludes naval tactics debate.
Covers Macedonian Wars (214–148 BCE), defeat of Seleucids, Corinth destruction; Greek cultural influence (Graecia capta); role of philhellenism among Roman elite; excludes detailed treaty terms.
Covers land reform, agrarian laws, assassination of Tiberius (133 BCE); death of Gaius (121 BCE); beginning of political violence; populares vs optimates; excludes full social context.
Covers Marius’ army reforms (professionalization); Sulla’s march on Rome, proscriptions, constitutional reforms; Mithridatic War; consequences for Republic; excludes detailed campaign maps.

units.n: Pompey, Crassus, Cicero & Catilinarian Conspiracy (63 BCE)
units.nt: Covers Pompey’s eastern campaigns, Crassus’ wealth, Cicero’s consulship, suppression of Catiline; First Triumvirate formation (60 BCE); excludes oratory analysis.
Covers secret alliance, Caesar’s Gallic Wars, death of Crassus, Senate vs Caesar; crossing the Rubicon; excludes detailed Gaul battle tactics.
Covers defeat of Pompey (Pharsalus 48 BCE); Egyptian affair (Cleopatra); calendar reform, dictatorship for life (44 BCE); assassination (Ides of March); excludes constitutional analysis.
Covers proscriptions, Battle of Philippi (42 BCE); division of provinces; Antony’s alliance with Cleopatra; rising conflict; excludes propaganda analysis.

units.n: Final Civil War: Actium (31 BCE) & Fall of Antony
units.nt: Covers naval battle, Cleopatra’s death, Antony’s suicide; Octavian’s sole rule; Egypt annexed as Roman province; excludes economic consequences.

Covers ‘Restoration of Republic’ (27 BCE); titles: Augustus, Princeps, Pontifex Maximus; division of provinces (imperial vs senatorial); reforms of army, treasury, and morals; excludes succession planning details.
Covers each emperor’s reign, key events (Germanicus, conquest of Britain, Great Fire); scandals, conspiracies; end with Nero’s suicide (68 CE); excludes detailed provincial policies.

units.n: Year of the Four Emperors (69 CE) & Flavian Dynasty
units.nt: Covers Galba, Otho, Vitellius, Vespasian; civil war; Flavian building projects (Colosseum); destruction of Jerusalem (70 CE); excludes Jewish War tactical details.

units.n: Five Good Emperors (Nerva to Marcus Aurelius)
units.nt: Covers adoptive succession: Trajan (max extent), Hadrian (walls, travel), Antoninus Pius (peace), Marcus Aurelius (Parthian/Marcomannic wars, Meditations); peak of Roman power; excludes philosophical analysis.

Covers Septimius Severus (military monarchy), Caracalla (Constitutio Antoniniana), Elagabalus, Alexander Severus; crisis of the third century: multiple emperors, invasions, plague, economic collapse; excludes detailed numismatics.
Covers division into East/West, two Augusti and two Caesares; economic reforms (Edict on Maximum Prices); military reorganization; abdication; excludes administrative boundary details.
Covers Battle of Milvian Bridge (312), Edict of Milan (313), Christian toleration; foundation of Constantinople (330); Council of Nicaea; army and monetary reforms; excludes theological debates.

units.n: Late Empire & Division after Theodosius (395 CE)
units.nt: Covers Valentinian, Valens, Adrianople (378); Theodosius I (last sole emperor); permanent East/West split; barbarian settlement; excludes ecclesiastical history.

units.n: Fall of the Western Roman Empire (410–476 CE)
units.nt: Covers Sack of Rome by Visigoths (410), by Vandals (455); last emperors Romulus Augustulus (deposed 476); Odoacer as King of Italy; reasons for fall (military, economic, political); excludes ‘Byzantine survival’ details.

units.n: Roman Law: Twelve Tables to Justinian’s Code
units.nt: Covers development of ius civile, praetorian edict, jurists (Gaius, Ulpian); Corpus Iuris Civilis (529–534 CE); legal concepts (paterfamilias, property, contracts); excludes detailed case law.
Covers denarius & sestertius; Mediterranean trade routes, amphorae, annona (grain dole); taxes (tributum, portoria); role of villas and latifundia; excludes full price history.

units.n: Social Hierarchy: Senators, Equestrians, Freedmen, Slaves
units.nt: Covers cursus honorum, property classes, patronage system (clientela); manumission; slavery as economic base (servile wars: Spartacus); daily life differences; excludes prosopographical databases.
Covers paterfamilias, patria potestas, marriage types (cum/manu vs sine manu); children’s education (ludus, rhetoric); role of women; excludes legal divorce cases.

units.n: Religion: State Cults, Mystery Religions & Imperial Cult
units.nt: Covers Capitoline Triad, pontiffs, augurs; foreign cults (Isis, Mithras); emperor worship as political loyalty; Christianity as outlawed cult; excludes detailed theology.
Covers concrete, arches, domes (Pantheon); aqueducts, roads (viae, milestones), bridges, basilicas, baths; examples: Colosseum, Circus Maximus; excludes modern engineering calculations.
Covers Golden Age: Cicero, Virgil (Aeneid), Horace, Ovid, Livy; Silver Age: Seneca, Tacitus, Pliny; Stoicism (Epictetus, Marcus); portraiture, frescoes (Pompeii); excludes textual criticism.
Covers legio (cohorts, centuries, legionaries), auxilia (archers, cavalry); ranks (centurion, legate); camp life, discipline (decimation); excludes equipment metallurgy details.
Covers gladius, pilum, scutum; manipular vs cohort tactics; testudo formation; siege engines (ballista, onager, siege towers); famous sieges (Alesia, Masada); excludes reconstruction specs.
Covers corvus, naval ram; fleet bases (Misenum, Ravenna); limes (Germanic, African, eastern frontiers), Hadrian’s Wall, Antonine Wall; Dacian wars; excludes minor fortlets.
Covers Constantinople as ‘New Rome’; reign of Justinian (527–565); reconquests under Belisarius; survival until 1453; Roman law legacy; excludes Orthodox theology.

units.n: Roman Cultural Legacy: Language, Law, Government
units.nt: Covers Latin as basis of Romance languages; Roman law as foundation for civil law (Napoleonic, German); republican ideals in Enlightenment and modern constitutions; Roman calendar and names; excludes reception studies.

Covers Polybius (pragmatic history), Livy (moralistic), Tacitus (critical of empire), Suetonius (biography), Cassius Dio (senatorial); archaeological and epigraphic evidence; modern debates; excludes full source criticism.
